note: Borrowed third-party statistics about production AI cost and reliability. Each row resolves to a primary, vendor, or survey source with a stated denominator and definition, and was citation-verified before publishing. Marked corroborating/methodology-varies, not first-party rigor — first-party numbers live in the benchmark spine. Vendor-run and disputed figures are flagged. Corrections welcome via PR against this file.

rows.metric: Share of RAG responses containing >=1 hallucinated span, data-to-text task (all 6 LLMs pooled)
rows.denominator: 6,198 model responses on the data-to-text task (1,033 source instances x 6 LLMs), human span-annotated
rows.failure_definition: Response contains at least one word-level span manually annotated as one of four hallucination types: Evident Conflict, Subtle Conflict, Evident Baseless Info, Subtle Baseless Info (content not supported by / contradicting the provided source)
rows.source_title: RAGTruth: A Hallucination Corpus for Developing Trustworthy Retrieval-Augmented Language Models (ACL 2024) — dataset stats, ParticleMedia/RAGTruth GitHub
rows.applicability: GPT-3.5/GPT-4 (0613), Llama-2-7B/13B/70B-chat, Mistral-7B-Instruct generating structured-data-to-text with gold source; data-to-text is the hardest of the 3 RAGTruth tasks
rows.notes: Percentage computed by us from the repo's verified counts (4,254 hallucination responses / 6,198 responses). QA task = 29.1% (1,724/5,934); summarization = 29.8% (1,686/5,658). Shows task type dominates hallucination rate.

rows.metric: Per-model overall RAG hallucination rate (share of that model's responses with >=1 hallucinated span)
rows.denominator: 2,965 source instances per model (17,790 total responses / 6 models = 2,965 each)
rows.failure_definition: Response contains >=1 human-annotated hallucination span (unsupported by or conflicting with the provided context), pooled across all 3 tasks
rows.source_title: RAGTruth dataset statistics — ParticleMedia/RAGTruth GitHub (ACL 2024 paper 2401.00396)
rows.applicability: 2023-era models on a fixed RAG corpus; absolute rates are inflated by the hard data-to-text task being 1/3 of the mix. Use for cross-model ranking, not as a 2026 production rate.
rows.notes: Per-model % computed by us from verified repo counts; total 17,790 responses / 6 models = 2,965 each. Stronger instruct models (GPT-4/3.5) hallucinate ~4-5x less than 7B-class open models on the same RAG inputs.

rows.metric: Hallucination rate on grounded summarization (summary contains facts absent from the source document)
rows.value: Llama-3.3-70B 4.1%; Gemini-2.5-Flash 7.8%; GPT-4o (2024-08-06) 9.6%; Claude-Opus-4.5 10.9%; Claude-Sonnet-4.5 12.0%
rows.denominator: >7,700 source articles/documents; each model summarizes each, judged by HHEM-2.3 factual-consistency model
rows.failure_definition: Summary is scored by HHEM-2.3 as factually inconsistent with the source — i.e., introduces information not present in (or contradicting) the provided passage. Only documents the model actually answered are scored (see answer rate).
rows.source_title: Vectara Hallucination Leaderboard (HHEM-2.3), GitHub
rows.applicability: Short-to-medium grounded summarization (the cleanest possible RAG: 1 correct doc supplied). Real lower bound — production retrieval adds wrong/missing docs on top. Leaderboard snapshot dated 2026-05-11.
rows.notes: Vendor-run (Vectara sells HHEM), so judge and benchmark share a maker — flagged contested. Answer rates 93.8-99.5%; refusals excluded from the rate. Exact per-model values drift as the live leaderboard updates.

rows.metric: Context length at which long-context RAG answer correctness peaks then degrades (per model)
rows.value: Llama-3.1-405B degrades after ~32K tokens; GPT-4-0125-preview after ~64K tokens; most of 13 models decline past a model-specific threshold
rows.denominator: >2,000 experiments across 13 open + closed LLMs on 4 RAG datasets: Databricks DocsQA, FinanceBench, HotPotQA, Natural Questions
rows.failure_definition: Drop in RAG answer correctness (LLM-graded answer quality vs. ground truth) as the number of retrieved tokens/documents in context increases, holding the retriever fixed
rows.source_title: Long Context RAG Performance of LLMs — Databricks Mosaic Research blog (also arXiv 2411.03538)
rows.applicability: Enterprise RAG where you increase retrieved-chunk count to raise recall; shows more context past a model-specific point hurts end-answer quality. Models tested as of late 2024.
rows.notes: Vendor (Databricks) but they ran the 2,000+ experiments and published the arXiv preprint; methodology is transparent. Could not directly fetch the blog (proxy 403) — values cross-confirmed via two independent search extractions of the same blog.

rows.metric: Distinct long-context failure modes: refusal/instruction-break rate rising with context length
rows.value: Claude-3-Sonnet copyright-refusal: 3.7% @16K -> 21% @32K -> 49.5% @64K. DBRX instruction-following failure: 5.2% @8K -> 17.6% @16K -> 50.4% @32K
rows.denominator: RAG questions across the 4 Databricks datasets, per model, bucketed by context length (same >2,000-experiment study)
rows.failure_definition: Claude: share of responses that refuse to answer citing copyright. DBRX: share of responses that fail to follow the answer-format instruction (e.g. summarizing instead of answering).
rows.source_title: Long Context RAG Performance of LLMs — Databricks Mosaic Research blog
rows.applicability: Specific to Claude-3-Sonnet and DBRX as of late 2024; illustrates that long-context RAG fails in model-specific, non-obvious ways (refusal, format drift) — not just lower accuracy.
rows.notes: Exact percentages confirmed via search extraction of the Databricks blog; direct fetch blocked by proxy policy on the host. Treat as model/version-specific, not a general law.

rows.metric: Hybrid retrieval + cross-encoder reranking lift over dense and over hybrid-alone (Recall@5), financial text-and-table QA
rows.value: Recall@5: Hybrid+Cohere-Rerank 0.816 vs Hybrid-RRF 0.695 (+17.4% rel) vs BM25 0.644 vs dense-only 0.587 (rerank is +39% rel over dense)
rows.denominator: 23,088 queries over 7,318 mixed text-and-table financial documents; 10 retrieval strategies; Recall@5/MRR/nDCG with paired bootstrap significance tests
rows.failure_definition: Recall@5 = fraction of queries whose gold-relevant chunk is within the top-5 retrieved (a miss = relevant chunk absent from top-5, which caps achievable answer quality)
rows.source_title: From BM25 to Corrective RAG: Benchmarking Retrieval Strategies for Text-and-Table Documents (EACL 2026)
rows.applicability: Financial documents with heavy tables — a domain where BM25 beats dense (0.644 vs 0.587), and reranking adds the largest single lift. Generalize the direction, not the exact numbers, to other table-heavy enterprise corpora.
rows.notes: Single-paper, single-corpus result; denominators are explicit. Confirms the BEIR-era pattern on a 2026 enterprise benchmark: dense alone is worst, hybrid+rerank best. arXiv abstract not directly fetchable (proxy 403) — numbers from search extraction of the paper.

rows.metric: GPT-4 (with plugins) task success rate on GAIA vs human baseline
rows.value: 15% (GPT-4 + plugins) vs 92% (human respondents)
rows.denominator: GAIA: 466 real-world general-assistant questions, with answers to 300 retained to power the public leaderboard; questions span 3 difficulty levels; each has exactly one correct answer.
rows.failure_definition: A task counts as success only on quasi-exact-match between the model's single final answer (string/number/list) and the ground truth, with type-tied normalization; no partial credit. Failure = any non-matching final answer.
rows.source_title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants (Mialon et al., Meta AI / Hugging Face)
rows.applicability: GPT-4-era agents (late 2023 baseline) on web-browsing + tool-use assistant tasks. Headline gap, not current SOTA — later models score far higher; cite as the original human-vs-AI gap, not 2026 performance.
rows.notes: Value surfaced via web search of the primary paper; direct fetch of arxiv.org was blocked by the environment proxy policy, so the source page was not opened in-session. Current leaderboard SOTA (~70s%) lives on secondary aggregators and is excluded (see cut_list).

rows.metric: GPT-4o single-trial task success rate (pass^1) on tau-bench, function calling
rows.value: GPT-4o pass^1: ~61% on tau-retail (61.2%), ~35% on tau-airline (35.2%)
rows.denominator: tau-bench: two domains of tool-agent-user customer-service tasks. Retail = 115 tasks; airline = 50 tasks (165 total). Each task is a multi-turn dialogue with API tools + policy rules, user simulated by an LM. pass^1 = success on a single i.i.d. trial.
rows.failure_definition: A task passes only if the final database/world state matches the annotated goal state AND policy is followed; pass^1 = success on a single i.i.d. trial. Failure = wrong end state or policy violation.
rows.source_title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains (Yao et al., Sierra)
rows.applicability: GPT-4o (2024) on realistic customer-service agent tasks with strict policy/tool use. Best-model-at-publication figure; not a production deployment measurement.
rows.notes: Per-domain N is approximate (paper reports ~115 airline / ~115+ retail tasks); treat exact N as 'order ~100-150 per domain' unless reading the paper's task table directly. Fetch of arxiv blocked by proxy; value from search of the primary paper + Sierra's own blog.

rows.metric: GPT-4o consistency / reliability on tau-bench-retail (pass^8 vs pass^1)
rows.value: pass^8 drops to ~25% (from 60.4% pass^1) — a ~60% relative drop
rows.denominator: tau-retail task set; pass^k = fraction of tasks solved correctly on ALL of k independent i.i.d. trials (here k=8). GPT-4o tool-calling (TC) agent. pass^1 = 0.604 per the sierra-research/tau-bench leaderboard.
rows.failure_definition: pass^k failure = the agent fails to reach the correct goal state on at least one of the 8 trials for a given task. Measures consistency, not average success.
rows.source_title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains (Yao et al., Sierra)
rows.applicability: GPT-4o (2024). This is the load-bearing reliability stat: even when average success is ~61%, repeatable success across 8 runs collapses below 25% — quantifies non-determinism/inconsistency of agents.
rows.notes: Value '<25%' is the figure stated in the paper/Sierra materials; exact decimal not pinned from the rendered table (arxiv fetch proxy-blocked).

rows.metric: Token usage of a multi-agent system vs. a single chat interaction
rows.value: ~15x more tokens
rows.denominator: Tokens consumed by Anthropic's internal multi-agent Research system per query vs. tokens consumed by a single Claude chat interaction (token-count ratio; Anthropic-internal measurement, no published N of queries)
rows.failure_definition: Not a failure event — a baseline cost-multiplier: how much more token spend a multi-step/multi-agent orchestration incurs relative to a one-shot chat, the structural driver of agent cost blowup
rows.source_title: How we built our multi-agent research system — Anthropic Engineering
rows.applicability: Anthropic Research multi-agent system (Claude orchestrator + subagents), mid-2025; generalizes qualitatively to other frameworks but exact 15x is Anthropic-specific
rows.notes: Same source: single agents use ~4x more tokens than chat. Verbatim Anthropic wording consistent across multiple independent quoters; anthropic.com blocked direct fetch in this environment but the figure is Anthropic's own published engineering data.

rows.metric: Share of agent performance variance explained by token usage alone (browsing eval)
rows.value: ~80% of variance (token count + tool calls + model = 95%; token count alone = 80%)
rows.denominator: Variance in task performance across runs on Anthropic's internal BrowseComp-style evaluation, regressed on token count, number of tool calls, and model choice
rows.failure_definition: Not a failure — quantifies that raw token spend (how much the agent 'thinks'/loops) is the dominant lever on agent performance, the reason uncapped agents trade money for marginal quality
rows.source_title: How we built our multi-agent research system — Anthropic Engineering
rows.applicability: Anthropic's internal browsing/research eval, mid-2025; not an independent reproduction
rows.notes: Anthropic-internal regression; links token blowup to agent behavior.

rows.metric: Cost/latency reduction from Anthropic prompt caching on long prompts
rows.value: up to 90% cost reduction; up to 85% latency reduction
rows.denominator: Per-request input-token cost for the cached (repeated-prefix) portion of a long prompt vs. the same tokens uncached; 'up to' = best case at high cache-hit ratio, not a workload average
rows.failure_definition: Not a failure — the headline savings ceiling for reusing a static prompt prefix (system prompt, docs, history) across calls, the standard mitigation for agent token blowup
rows.source_title: Prompt caching with Claude — Anthropic (launch announcement, Aug 2024)
rows.applicability: Claude 3.5 Sonnet / 3 Opus / 3 Haiku at launch (Aug 2024); 'up to' figures are ceilings — realized bill reduction depends on cache-hit rate and output-token share
rows.notes: Vendor 'up to' ceiling — flag the gap vs. realized savings. The 90% per-token figure is confirmed by the pricing mechanism in the claude-cache-multiplier row.

rows.metric: Claude cache-read token price as a multiple of base input token price
rows.value: cache read = 0.1x base input (90% cheaper); 5-min cache write = 1.25x; 1-hour cache write = 2x
rows.denominator: Per-token API price multiplier, cache-read vs base-input tokens, across the Claude model line (e.g. Opus 4.8 input $5/MTok -> cache read $0.50/MTok, 5m write $6.25/MTok, 1h write $10/MTok)
rows.failure_definition: Not a failure — the actual billing mechanism behind the 90% caching claim; sets break-even (pays off after 1 read at 5-min TTL, 2 reads at 1-hour TTL)
rows.source_title: Prompt caching — Claude Platform Docs (pricing multipliers)
rows.applicability: Current Claude API pricing as of June 2026; fetched directly from Claude docs
rows.notes: Directly fetched and quoted from the docs — the verifiable mechanism. The 90%-bill-reduction headline only holds at high hit-rate on input-dominated workloads.

rows.metric: Cost/latency reduction from OpenAI Prompt Caching (automatic)
rows.value: 50% discount on cached input tokens (GPT-4o family at launch, Oct 2024); up to ~80% lower time-to-first-token latency on cache hits
rows.denominator: Cached-input-token price vs. base input price (50% off), auto-applied to prompts >1,024 tokens sharing a common prefix; latency = TTFT on cache-hit vs. cache-miss
rows.failure_definition: Not a failure — OpenAI's competing caching discount; the baseline mitigation savings on the OpenAI side (no opt-in, GPT-4o family and later)
rows.source_title: Prompt Caching in the API — OpenAI (Oct 2024)
rows.applicability: GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, o1-preview, o1-mini and fine-tunes at launch (Oct 2024); discount is 50% (later flagship models reportedly higher — not confirmed to an OpenAI primary URL here)
rows.notes: Launch-page figure is 50%; secondary blogs claiming OpenAI now matches Anthropic's 90% were not confirmed to an OpenAI primary URL, so the conservative 50% is reported.

rows.metric: Effective context length under latent (non-literal) retrieval; share of models falling below 50% of their short-context base score at 32K
rows.value: 10 of the evaluated models drop below 50% of their short-context base at 32K; GPT-4o falls from 99.3% base to 81.6% at 32K (effective length 8K, i.e. first drop below 85% of base by 16K); Llama-3.3-70B 97.3%->59.5%; Gemini-1.5-Pro 92.6%->55.5%
rows.denominator: NoLiMa needle-in-haystack with minimal lexical overlap between question and needle (requires latent association); haystacks scaled from <1K to 32K tokens; main results table covers ~22 models that each claim >=128K context.
rows.failure_definition: Accuracy on locating/answering from the single needle; 'effective length' = longest context where a model still scores >=85% of its own <1K base score.
rows.source_title: NoLiMa: Long-Context Evaluation Beyond Literal Matching (Modarressi et al., ICML 2025)
rows.applicability: 12-13 frontier LLMs claiming >=128K context (GPT-4o, Llama-3.3-70B, Gemini-1.5-Pro, etc.); semantic/latent retrieval, not literal keyword match. 2025.
rows.notes: Per-model figures read from the official Adobe Research GitHub results table. WebSearch summaries variously said '11 models' / '13 models' / 'below 50% at 32K'; the repo's own wording ('at 32K, 10 models drop below 50% of their strong short-length base') is used. Effective-length values (GPT-4o=8K) are far below advertised 128K+.

rows.metric: Effective vs advertised context length (synthetic retrieval/multi-hop/aggregation)
rows.value: Only ~half of models effectively handle 32K; effective lengths: GPT-4-1106 = 64K, Llama-3.1-70B = 64K, Command-R-plus = 32K, Qwen2-72B = 32K, DBRX = 8K, Qwen1.5 = 8K — all below claimed 32K+ windows
rows.denominator: RULER synthetic suite (13 task types incl. multi-needle NIAH, multi-hop variable tracing, aggregation) over context lengths 4K-128K. Original COLM 2024 paper evaluated 10 long-context LLMs; the current public GitHub leaderboard has expanded to 34 models (1.2B-235B params, incl. GPT-4, Gemini-1.5, Llama, Qwen, Command-R, DBRX).
rows.failure_definition: 'Effective length' = longest context at which a model still exceeds the qualitative threshold set by Llama-2-7B's score at 4K (85.6%).
rows.source_title: RULER: What's the Real Context Size of Your Long-Context Language Models? (Hsieh et al., NVIDIA, COLM 2024)
rows.applicability: GPT-4, Gemini-1.5-Pro, and 15+ open models (Llama, Qwen, Command-R, DBRX, Jamba). Synthetic retrieval/tracing/aggregation. 2024, repo updated with more models since.
rows.notes: Paper/repo: 'all models claim 32K+, only half can effectively handle 32K... almost all fall below the threshold before reaching their claimed lengths.' Model count cited as 10 in original paper, expanded to 34 in current repo leaderboard.

rows.metric: Performance gap between focused (~300-token) and full (~113K-token) input on the same questions (context rot)
rows.value: 30-60% performance gap; every one of the 18 models scored dramatically higher on the focused ~300-token version than the full ~113K-token version
rows.denominator: LongMemEval conversational QA: 113,000-token conversation history vs a 'focused' version with only the ~300 relevant tokens; same questions; 18 frontier models (GPT-4.1, Claude 4 Opus/Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Pro/Flash, Qwen3, etc.).
rows.failure_definition: Question-answering accuracy on the target event; the gap isolates pure input-length effect since the relevant 300 tokens are identical in both conditions.
rows.source_title: Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance (Hong, Troynikov, Huber — Chroma, July 2025)
rows.applicability: 18 frontier models, mid-2025 (GPT-4.1, Claude Opus 4 / Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5, Qwen3). Conversational long-memory QA.
rows.notes: Vendor (Chroma, a vector-DB company with an interest in RAG/short-context). Method and toolkit are open at github.com/chroma-core/context-rot. Figures (30-60% gap, 113K vs 300 tokens, 18 models) corroborated across multiple independent summaries; primary page was 403 to WebFetch but readable via WebSearch and the GitHub README confirms authors/tasks.

rows.metric: Effect of needle-question semantic similarity and distractors on retrieval as context grows
rows.value: Lower needle-question similarity and added distractors accelerate accuracy collapse as length grows; degradation is non-uniform with length even on simple NIAH (vs near-perfect at short length)
rows.denominator: Extended NIAH: needle-question similarity controlled, 1-4 distractors added, haystack structure varied; 18 frontier models; lengths from short to ~tens of thousands of tokens.
rows.failure_definition: Correct extraction of the needle answer; measured as accuracy decline across input lengths for each similarity/distractor condition.
rows.source_title: Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance (Chroma, July 2025)
rows.applicability: 18 frontier models, 2025. Single-needle semantic retrieval with controlled distractors.
rows.notes: Directional/qualitative finding (exact per-condition percentages not pinned to a quotable number here). Vendor source; toolkit open-sourced. Kept because the mechanism (similarity- and distractor-driven decay) is the load-bearing claim, not a specific %.

rows.metric: Accuracy degradation from input length alone, with perfect/forced retrieval (no distraction)
rows.value: 13.9%-85% performance drop as input length increases despite perfect retrieval — observed even when irrelevant tokens are replaced with whitespace and even when they are masked so the model attends only to the relevant tokens; all within the models' claimed context windows
rows.denominator: Controlled experiments on 5 LLMs (2 open: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-v0.3-7B-Instruct; 3 closed: GPT-4o, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, Gemini-2.0) across math (GSM8K), QA (MMLU), coding (HumanEval), and a synthetic variable-summation task; irrelevant tokens replaced with whitespace or attention-masked so only relevant tokens are attendable.
rows.failure_definition: Task accuracy (correct final answer/solution) as a function of total input length when retrieval is held perfect — isolates length itself from retrieval/distraction.
rows.source_title: Context Length Alone Hurts LLM Performance Despite Perfect Retrieval (Du et al., Findings of EMNLP 2025)
rows.applicability: 5 LLMs (open + closed); math/QA/coding; lengths within models' claimed windows. 2025.
rows.notes: Strongest evidence that length per se (not just lost-in-the-middle or distractors) degrades accuracy. Reciting retrieved evidence first recovers up to ~4% on RULER for GPT-4o. Wide 13.9-85% range spans tasks/models.

rows.metric: Share of respondents who have implemented observability for their agents
rows.value: ~89% (stated as "nearly 89%")
rows.denominator: All survey respondents in LangChain's "State of Agent Engineering" report (~1,300+; 1,340 responses fielded Nov 18–Dec 2, 2025). Note: report uses "respondents" and "organizations" interchangeably for this figure.
rows.failure_definition: 'Observability' = respondent reports having implemented some form of observability/monitoring mechanism for their agents (any form, including basic logging up to full tracing). Self-reported.
rows.source_title: LangChain — State of Agent Engineering
rows.applicability: Self-selected audience of agent builders/engineers in LangChain's network; late-2025/2026 report. Skews toward teams already building agents, so likely overstates observability adoption vs. the general developer population.
rows.notes: Primary langchain.com page returned HTTP 403 to the fetcher; value is consistently quoted by multiple independent secondaries (KDnuggets, multiple search-indexed summaries). source_type marked 'vendor' because the underlying survey is a LangChain marketing report.

rows.metric: Share of respondents conducting offline evaluations (evals on test sets)
rows.denominator: All respondents to LangChain's "State of Agent Engineering" survey (1,340 responses; framed as 1,300+ professionals), public survey run Nov 18–Dec 2, 2025
rows.failure_definition: 'Offline evals' = respondent reports running evaluations on a fixed test set / dataset (pre-deployment, not on live production traffic). Self-reported.
rows.source_title: LangChain — State of Agent Engineering
rows.applicability: Same audience as above. The widely-cited '52% run evals' headline specifically refers to OFFLINE evals (52.4%) — not all evals. Important: the 89%-vs-52% gap contrasts observability (any form) with offline evals only.
rows.notes: Corroborated as '52.4% conducting offline evaluations' and as 'only 52.4% are conducting offline evaluations' across multiple summaries of the report. This is the load-bearing 'evals vs observability' contrast.

rows.metric: Share with detailed tracing (inspect individual agent steps / tool calls)
rows.value: 62% overall; 71.5% among orgs with agents in production
rows.denominator: All respondents (62%) vs. subset with agents in production (71.5%)
rows.failure_definition: 'Detailed tracing' = observability deep enough to inspect individual agent steps and tool calls (a stronger bar than basic 'some form of observability').
rows.source_title: LangChain — State of Agent Engineering
rows.applicability: Same audience. Distinguishes 'any observability' (~89%) from 'full tracing' (62%) — useful because not all observability is equal.
rows.notes: Quoted alongside the 94% / 71.5% production-subset figures. Confidence medium pending primary-source confirmation (403).

rows.metric: AI monitoring adoption (organizations monitoring AI/ML workloads)
rows.value: 54% in 2025, up from 42% in 2024
rows.denominator: Over 1,700 IT and engineering leaders/team members across 23 countries and 11 industries (New Relic 2025 Observability Forecast)
rows.failure_definition: Respondent organization reports having adopted/utilizing monitoring of AI workloads (AI monitoring as an observability capability). Broader than agent/LLM evals specifically.
rows.source_title: New Relic — 2025 Observability Forecast
rows.applicability: General IT/engineering leaders (not LLM/agent-specialist audience), 23 countries / 11 industries, 2025. Measures 'monitoring of AI workloads' broadly — NOT the evals-vs-observability split for LLM/agent teams. Use as a directional adjacent datapoint only.
rows.notes: N (~1,700) and the 42%→54% YoY figure corroborated across New Relic press materials and APMdigest. Included for the observability-adoption angle; does not speak to evals.

rows.metric: Predicted share of GenAI projects abandoned after proof of concept by end of 2025
rows.value: At least 30%
rows.denominator: Generative-AI projects in enterprises (Gartner prediction, announced at Gartner Data & Analytics Summit Sydney, 30 July 2024)
rows.failure_definition: Project abandoned after the proof-of-concept stage, attributed to poor data quality, inadequate risk controls, escalating costs, or unclear business value
rows.source_title: Gartner Predicts 30% of Generative AI Projects Will Be Abandoned After Proof of Concept By End of 2025
rows.applicability: Forward-looking prediction for end-of-2025; enterprise GenAI projects globally
rows.notes: This is a forecast, not measured outcome. Attributed to Rita Sallam (Distinguished VP Analyst). Gartner newsroom URL 403s the fetcher but the figure and wording are quoted verbatim across APMdigest, HPCwire, IntelligentCIO, Quantexa.

rows.metric: Reported share of GenAI projects abandoned after proof of concept (retrospective)
rows.value: Over 50% (abandoned after PoC by the end of 2025)
rows.denominator: Hundreds of GenAI implementations Gartner analyzed (no public N or sampling frame disclosed)
rows.failure_definition: Abandoned after the proof-of-concept stage, due to poor data quality, insufficient risk controls, rising costs, or unclear business value
rows.source_title: Why 50% of GenAI Projects Fail — And How to Beat the Odds (Gartner)
rows.applicability: Retrospective claim covering through end of 2024; enterprise GenAI
rows.notes: Contested/lower-confidence: this 'over 50%' supersedes the earlier 30% prediction but the public article + Gartner's own X/Threads posts describe the base only as 'hundreds of GenAI implementations' — no published N or denominator definition. Treat as directional, not a clean rate.

rows.metric: Share of companies abandoning the MAJORITY of their AI initiatives before they reach production
rows.value: 42% (up from 17% the prior year)
rows.denominator: 1,006 midlevel and senior IT and line-of-business professionals (North America and Europe) in S&P Global Market Intelligence / 451 Research 'Voice of the Enterprise: AI & Machine Learning, Use Cases 2025' survey
rows.failure_definition: Company abandons the majority of its AI initiatives before they reach production deployment (i.e., they do not progress past pilot/PoC into broad use)
rows.source_title: Generative AI shows rapid growth but yields mixed results (S&P Global Market Intelligence / 451 Research)
rows.applicability: Enterprise AI initiatives, 2025; North America + Europe; respondent-reported
rows.notes: Strong primary survey with named instrument and N. spglobal.com page 403s the fetcher but the 17%→42% figure and n=1,006 are quoted verbatim across CIO Dive, Telecom Reseller, WorkOS. Self-reported abandonment (org-level), distinct from MIT's P&L measure.

rows.metric: Average share of AI projects scrapped between proof of concept and broad adoption
rows.value: 46% (average share of an organization's AI projects scrapped between proof of concept and broad adoption)
rows.denominator: S&P Global Market Intelligence / 451 Research 'Voice of the Enterprise: AI & Machine Learning, Use Cases 2025'; survey of 1,006 midlevel and senior IT and line-of-business professionals across North America and Europe
rows.failure_definition: AI projects that are scrapped/discontinued somewhere between the proof-of-concept stage and broad/production adoption
rows.source_title: Generative AI shows rapid growth but yields mixed results (S&P Global Market Intelligence / 451 Research)
rows.applicability: Enterprise AI projects, 2025; North America + Europe; respondent-reported average
rows.notes: This is the per-organization average attrition rate across the PoC→adoption funnel, the cleaner 'pilot-to-production gap' number from the same study. Same sourcing caveat as the 42% row.
